questions.question: What is the main content of the four Vedas composed during the Early Vedic period?
questions.solution: The four Vedas—Rigveda, Samaveda, Yajurveda, and Atharvaveda—form the foundation of the Vedic religion. Their primary content consists of prayers and hymns (Suktas) addressed to various deities like Indra, Agni, and Varuna, seeking their blessings for prosperity, health, and victory in war.

questions.question: Which of the following Jain councils was held at Vallabhi (Gujarat) in 512 CE under the chairmanship of Devardhi Kshamasramana?
questions.solution: The Second Jain Council was held at Vallabhi in Gujarat around 512 CE. It was presided over by Devardhi Kshamasramana. The main objective of this council was to collect the Jain religious texts (Agamas) and commit them to writing to ensure their preservation for future generations.

questions.question: Which site provides archaeological evidence of the earliest known urban civilisation in the Indian subcontinent with planned city structures?
questions.solution: Harappa, discovered in 1921, was the first site to be excavated, giving the Indus Valley Civilisation its alternate name. It provides clear evidence of a sophisticated urban civilisation characterized by a grid system of streets, fortified citadels, and standardized brick sizes.

questions.question: During the Gupta period, who was the Commander-in-Chief of the Gupta Army?
questions.solution:
In the Gupta administrative and military hierarchy, the Commander-in-Chief was known as the Mahasenapati. Other important officials included:

  • Sandhivigrahika: Minister of peace and war (Foreign Minister).
  • Mahamatya: High-ranking official or counselor.
  • Amatya: General term for administrative officers or civil servants.

questions.question: During which of the following dynasties was the Kailasa Temple at Ellora built?
questions.solution: The Kailasa Temple (Cave 16) at Ellora is a masterpiece of rock-cut architecture. It was built during the 8th century by the Rashtrakuta king Krishna I. The entire temple was carved out of a single monolithic basalt cliff.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Mauryan Empire and established a centralised administrative system?
questions.solution: Chandragupta Maurya, with the assistance of his mentor Chanakya (Kautilya), overthrew the Nanda dynasty to found the Mauryan Empire. He established a highly centralised administrative system that controlled a vast territory, stretching from modern-day Afghanistan to the Deccan.

questions.question: After the fall of Mauryas, which dynasty was established by Pushyamitra Shunga, a commander-in-chief who killed the last Mauryan?
questions.solution: After the decline of the Mauryas, Pushyamitra Shunga, the Senapati (Commander-in-Chief), assassinated the last Mauryan ruler, Brihadratha, in 185 BCE. He then established the Shunga Dynasty, which marked a period of Brahmanical revival in North India.

questions.question: The primary cause for the rise of Jainism and Buddhism was the religious unrest in India in the ______.
questions.solution: The 6th century BCE is often called the period of 'Great Transformation' or 'Second Urbanisation' in India. Religious unrest against complex Vedic rituals, the rigid varna system, and animal sacrifices led to the rise of heterodox sects, most notably Jainism and Buddhism.

questions.question: Which of the following dynasties initiated the practice of land grants to Brahmanas?
questions.solution: The Satavahanas (late 2nd century BCE to early 3rd century CE) are credited with being the first dynasty in India to initiate the practice of granting tax-free land to Brahmanas and Buddhist monks. This practice eventually paved the way for the development of Indian feudalism.

questions.question: ‘Milind Panho’ is a post-Mauryan text which contains the conversations between the Indo-Greek ruler Menander and the scholar and Buddhist monk ___________.
questions.solution: Milind Panho (Questions of Milinda) is a famous Buddhist text written in Pali. It records a philosophical dialogue between the Indo-Greek King Menander I (Milinda) and the Buddhist sage Nagasena, which eventually led to the king's conversion to Buddhism.

questions.question: Which of the following kings of Kalinga in the 2ⁿᵈ century B.C. professed Jainism in India?
questions.solution: Kharavela was the greatest king of the Mahameghavahana dynasty of Kalinga. According to the Hathigumpha Inscription, he was a devout follower of Jainism and was responsible for building many residential caves for Jain monks at Udayagiri and Khandagiri hills.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a reason for the Paleolithic people to move from place to place?
questions.solution: The Paleolithic people were hunter-gatherers who led a nomadic life. They moved due to resource exhaustion, following the movement of prey, and searching for seasonal plants. Failure of crops was NOT a factor because agriculture had not yet been discovered; it only began during the Neolithic period.

questions.question: What was the other name of Chandragupta II, the renowned ruler of the Gupta dynasty?
questions.solution: Chandragupta II, the son of Samudragupta, is famously known by the title Vikramaditya. His reign is considered the 'Golden Age' of the Gupta Empire, noted for the presence of the Navaratnas (nine gems), including the poet Kalidasa, in his court.

questions.question: Which of the following is cited as evidence for a highly centralised administration in the Indus-Sarasvati civilisation?
questions.option_3: Absence of seals and standard script
questions.solution: The use of standardised weights and measures across a vast geographical area (from Afghanistan to Gujarat) is strong evidence of a centralised administration in the Indus Valley. The uniform ratio of brick dimensions (1:2:4) also points toward a high degree of regulatory control.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are an ancient set of inscriptions, promoting moral values and governance principles and is considered one of the earliest written records in Indian history?
questions.solution: The Edicts of Ashoka are a collection of 33 inscriptions on pillars, boulders, and cave walls. They represent the first tangible evidence of Buddhism and outline Ashoka's policy of Dhamma, emphasizing non-violence, social responsibility, and ethical governance.

questions.question: At what age did Gautama Buddha attain Enlightenment (Nirvana) at Bodh Gaya in Bihar?
questions.solution: Siddhartha Gautama left his home at the age of 29 (Mahabhinishkramana). After six years of rigorous penance and meditation, he attained Enlightenment (Nirvana) at the age of 35 under a Pipal tree (Bodhi tree) in Bodh Gaya.

questions.question: During the Later Vedic period, which of the following animals was specifically mentioned as useful for ploughing swampy land?
questions.solution: While the Rigveda mentions cattle as primary wealth, the Later Vedic texts show a greater focus on agriculture. The buffalo was specifically recognized for its strength and ability to work in swampy or marshy lands, making it valuable for expanded cultivation in the Gangetic plains.

questions.question: Which military division had become rare in warfare during the Gupta period despite being part of the army in texts?
questions.solution: Ancient Indian armies traditionally followed the Chaturanga (four-fold) division: infantry, cavalry, elephants, and chariots. By the Gupta period, the use of chariots had become almost obsolete or rare in actual combat, giving way to more mobile cavalry and powerful elephant corps.

questions.question: During the Indus Valley Civilisation, the presence of jewellery made from precious metals and semi-precious stones in small-town settings supports the argument that ____.
questions.option_2: there was an equal access to material goods
questions.option_3: only the elite had access to luxury items.
questions.option_4: the civilisation was ruled by autocratic priest-kings
questions.solution: The uneven distribution and presence of high-value items like gold and semi-precious stone jewelry in varied urban settings suggest strong social stratification. It indicates that different classes of people had varying levels of wealth and status within the Indus Valley society.

questions.question: Which of the following groups of people is said to have migrated into the late Indus Valley era India from Central Asia?
questions.solution: The Aryan Migration Theory suggests that Indo-Aryan speaking groups migrated from Central Asia into the Indian subcontinent during the late stages of the Indus Valley Civilisation (around 1500 BCE), bringing with them the early forms of the Sanskrit language and Vedic culture.

questions.question: The Visuddhimagga, a key Buddhist text, was written by which of the following scholars?
questions.solution: The Visuddhimagga (The Path of Purification) is a landmark treatise on Theravada Buddhist doctrine. It was written by the great scholar-monk Buddhaghosa in Sri Lanka during the 5th century CE.

questions.question: Which Indo-Greek ruler's envoy Heliodorus installed a pillar in Vidisha and was a devotee of Lord Krishna?
questions.solution: Heliodorus was an ambassador of the Indo-Greek King Antialkidas to the court of King Bhagabhadra. He erected the Besnagar Pillar (Garuda Pillar) in Vidisha, Madhya Pradesh, identifying himself as a 'Bhagavata' (devotee of Vishnu/Krishna).

questions.question: The famous male torso from Harappa, remarkable for its naturalistic pose and chiselled in the round, is made of ___________.
questions.solution: One of the most famous sculptures from Harappa is a male torso carved from red limestone. It is celebrated for its highly realistic and naturalistic rendering of the human body, featuring sockets for the attachment of the head and arms.

questions.question: During which period of Indian history were the stupas of Sanchi and Sarnath built, reflecting its architectural style?
questions.solution: Major architectural contributions like the Great Stupa at Sanchi (originally commissioned) and the Dharmarajika Stupa were initiated during the Mauryan rule under Emperor Ashoka. Though they were expanded and renovated by later dynasties, their primary architectural identity is rooted in the Mauryan era.

questions.question: The Mauryan empire was founded by Chandragupta Maurya in which of the following years?
questions.solution: The Mauryan Empire was founded in 322 BCE. This followed Chandragupta Maurya's victory over the Nanda King Dhana Nanda and his successful resistance against the Macedonian garrisons left behind by Alexander the Great.
